extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) is VIOLATED.

The danger is transition t40: on send_attempt if (opted_out and stage >= 12): sent := true. This sets sent=true while opted_out is true, directly contradicting the safety property. Since stage is bounded in [0,12], the guard stage >= 12 is satisfiable only at stage == 12, so the question reduces to whether stage 12 is reachable.

Stage 12 is reachable via a fixed key "unlock code" through the forward chain of transitions:
0 --key_0--> 1 (t3); 1 --key_1--> 2 (t6); 2 --key_1--> 3 (t9); 3 --key_1--> 4 (t12); 4 --key_1--> 5 (t15); 5 --key_1--> 6 (t18); 6 --key_2--> 7 (t21); 7 --key_0--> 8 (t24); 8 --key_0--> 9 (t27); 9 --key_0--> 10 (t30); 10 --key_2--> 11 (t33); 11 --key_0--> 12 (t36). That is 12 key events, all while opted_out=false and sent=false.

Then optout (t1) sets opted_out=true, sent=false, stage stays 12. Then send_attempt: t2 is disabled (opted_out is true), t39 is disabled (stage is not < 12), so t40 fires and sets sent=true while opted_out=true. After this 14th event the state is opted_out=true, sent=true, stage=12 — a reachable state where the invariant fails.

Total: 14 events, within the 15-event bound. I implemented the exact transition semantics (per-event mutually exclusive guards, unchanged state when no transition is enabled — note stage 12 has no outgoing key transitions, so it is absorbing for keys) in a simulator and confirmed the final state is opted_out=true, sent=true. The witness event sequence is: key_0, key_1, key_1, key_1, key_1, key_1, key_2, key_0, key_0, key_0, key_2, key_0, optout, send_attempt.
